themes.summary: Micron's strong earnings and long-term agreements signal a structural shift in memory from cyclical commodity to scarce AI infrastructure, with multi-year take-or-pay contracts, price floors, and customer deposits, driving significant ASP increases and high gross margins for DRAM, HBM, and NAND. This scarcity is expected to persist beyond 2027, benefiting memory peers like SK Hynix, Samsung, SanDisk, and Kioxia.

themes.topic: AI Infrastructure Compute Bottleneck & Hyperscaler Capex
themes.summary: AI infrastructure is facing a system-level compute bottleneck, extending beyond GPUs to HBM, KV cache, CXL, networking, and CPU scheduling. Hyperscalers are committing to multi-year take-or-pay agreements for memory and other bottleneck components, leading to increased capex, cash commitments, and procurement risk, but also strengthening competitive positions for those who secure supply.

Advanced packaging, particularly CoWoS-like capacity, has become a major bottleneck for AI accelerators. Intel is strategically attacking TSMC not through leading-node competition, but by leveraging its EMIB and next-generation EMIB-T technologies to build a foothold in AI packaging and outsourced semiconductor assembly.

themes.topic: Foundry Pricing Power and Capacity Expansion
themes.summary: TSMC is raising prices by 5-10% on all leading-edge 7nm and below chip manufacturing processes, which account for ~75% of its revenue, indicating strong demand. South Korea is also in talks with Samsung and SK Hynix to accelerate chip fab construction to meet "exponential and explosive" AI demand.

Photonics is anticipated as the next supercycle, with major revenue ramps expected from mid-2027. The industry needs increased manufacturing capacity and test infrastructure, with companies like AAOI investing in 6-inch InP transitions. Poet/Sivers are also positioned to power top-3 hyperscalers with CPO/NPO solutions.

The AI model layer is dominated by an Anthropic-OpenAI duopoly, with both experiencing rapid revenue growth from agent workflows and enterprise contracts. Chinese vendors like ByteDance, DeepSeek, and GLM-5.2 are accelerating, commoditizing mid-tier tasks, while the overall sector shifts towards ROI discipline in token pricing and model routing.

themes.topic: Custom AI ASICs and Market Dynamics
themes.summary: The custom AI ASIC market is active, with Intel-backed SambaNova seeking significant funding at a high valuation. OpenAI's collaboration with Broadcom ($AVGO) on new chips is impacting competitors like Cerebras, highlighting the competitive landscape in custom AI silicon.

themes.topic: Agentic AI Driving Server CPU Demand
themes.summary: Agentic AI workloads are structurally reshaping data centers, expanding demand beyond accelerator-only racks to include CPU racks for control plane and program execution. This creates incremental demand for x86 CPUs (AMD, Intel), Nvidia Grace, and power-efficient Arm-based data center CPUs (Qualcomm, Arm).

themes.topic: PCB Industry Growth from AI Demand
themes.summary: Taiwan's Printed Circuit Board (PCB) output value is expected to rise 15.1% to NT$1.05 trillion this year, surpassing the NT$T mark for the first time, driven by strong AI-related demand.

Demand for rare earth magnets (NdFeB) is skyrocketing due to their use in robotics, indicating a growing trend in physical AI applications. Hyperscalers like Amazon are also exploring robotics for internal operational optimization.

themes.topic: Sustained Demand for GPUs and AI Accelerators
themes.summary: The memory supercycle validates multi-year demand for GPUs and AI accelerator systems, with HBM supply being a core input. Competitive differentiation in AI silicon increasingly depends on memory architecture, HBM allocation, and packaging integration.

themes.topic: Datacenter Power and Energy Storage Needs
themes.summary: The increasing compute demands for AI are leading to significant power requirements for datacenters, with Anthropic securing incremental capacity from SpaceX Colossus, involving over 220k NVIDIA GPUs and more than 300MW of power.
Discussion around MLCCs and their materials suggests attention on this component sector, likely driven by broader electronics and AI infrastructure demand.

Taiwan's export orders in May rose 47.2% year-on-year to US$89.5 billion, the second highest single month ever, led by IT/Communications (including servers) and Electronics (including chips), indicating robust global demand for tech components.
